title: Carved cherry dessert spoon
subtitle: A green-cherry spoon with a shallow oval bowl, shaped with axe and hook knife
excerpt: Cherry is the wood that rewards patience: it carves more slowly than sycamore green, but the finished surface takes a warm amber tone that sycamore never matches, and the grain holds the hook-knife bowl to a fine, even wall. This intermediate project covers the axe-to-knife progression, a shallow round bowl in a medium hook knife, and a raw-linseed cure.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the axe-and-knife method on small domestic implements in green hardwoods. Beeler, The Tools That Built America (1976) for the cherry-wood tradition in American domestic treen.

content.text: Cherry resists the knife more than sycamore but gives a dessert spoon worth keeping. The grain is tight and the surface finishes close, so a light 180-grit sand before oiling brings up a surface that looks almost polished. The finished spoon takes on a warm amber colour as the raw linseed oil sets; after a few months of use it deepens further toward the red-brown that makes cherry treen distinctive. This project uses
green wood

rather than seasoned: green cherry is workable under hand tools, whereas seasoned cherry is hard enough to need a gouge or lathe. Apple and plum have very similar properties and are often more accessible from orchard prunings or windfall.
What you need: wood

A freshly riven cherry billet, 22 to 25 cm long, 6 to 7 cm across the widest face. Cherry is less commonly grown for coppice than sycamore, but orchards, community fruit-growing groups, and tree surgeons are reliable sources. The wood must be freshly cut and green: a fresh axe cut across the end grain should darken from pale yellow to orange-brown within a minute. If the cut stays pale, the wood has already dried beyond the workable green stage.
What you need: tools
A carving axe for roughing the profile. A sloyd knife for the profile refinement and back-of-bowl shaping. A small-radius
hook knife
for the shallow bowl hollow. A leather
strop
for both knives; strop the hook knife every ten minutes of hollowing. A 1,000-grit waterstone for rebuilding the hook-knife edge if it chips on the harder green cherry grain.
Safety

Four-finger grip on the work at all times. Hook-knife hollowing requires the chest-brace: elbow against the chest, blade-arc unable to reach the body. Strop every ten minutes; cherry work dulls a hook-knife edge faster than sycamore.
The work
Step 1: Axe the rough profile

Mark the spoon profile on the flat face of the billet. A dessert spoon has a smaller oval bowl than an eating spoon: 8 cm by 4 cm, with a 12 cm handle. Rough out with the carving axe, staying 1 cm outside the pencilled line. The smaller profile means less axe work than a serving spoon; a few minutes of axe shaping takes the
blank
to within push-cut range.
Step 2: Refine the profile and back of bowl

Take push cuts along the outline to bring the blank to the pencilled line. The back of the bowl is a shallow dome: 5 mm proud of the handle plane at the deepest point of the dome. Work the dome with push cuts that radiate from the rim toward the bowl centre, all the way round. Cherry grain reads carefully here: any cut that lifts a chip and tears rather than peeling cleanly is going against the grain; reverse direction immediately.
Step 3: Hollow the bowl with the hook knife

A dessert-spoon bowl is shallow: 6 to 8 mm deep at the centre, with walls 3 to 4 mm thick. Hold the blank bowl-up in the four-finger grip, hook knife in the dominant hand with the blade curving over the bowl rim. The small-radius hook knife cuts a tighter, shallower arc than a medium or large knife; it is the right choice for this small bowl. Work from the centre outward in short pull strokes, rotating the spoon between passes. Check the wall thickness by holding the blank up to a light: thin walls appear slightly translucent. Keep them even.
Step 4: Refine the handle

The dessert-spoon handle is slender: oval in section, 12 mm by 8 mm at the mid-grip, narrowing to the neck at 8 mm by 6 mm. Take push cuts along the handle to bring it to section, then refine with short thumb-pivot cuts. The handle tip can be carved to a slight widened terminal or left as a simple rounded taper.
Step 5: Dry

Leave in a warm ventilated room for a week. Cherry dries to its characteristic amber. End-grain checking is common on cherry; a small check at the tip or handle end is normal and does not weaken the spoon.
Finishing

Sand lightly along the grain at 180 grit, then wipe with a damp cloth and allow to dry for ten minutes (the moisture raises the grain fibres; the second sand at 180 grit after drying removes them cleanly). Apply raw linseed oil, work in with a cloth, leave one hour, wipe off completely. Cure for two weeks. Cherry deepens in colour every time it is oiled; after several re-coats over the first year it will be a warm red-brown.
Variations
Apple and plum have the same tight grain and colour warmth as cherry. Apple is denser; the finished spoon is heavier and more resilient but takes longer to carve green.
A set of four matching dessert spoons from the same branch has a consistent grain pattern. Work them in sequence from the same billet rather than from separate pieces.
Left-handed hook knives have the curve opening to the left. The bowl hollowing is otherwise the same; the wrist rotation reverses.
Care
Hand wash in warm water, towel dry at once. No dishwasher, no soaking. Re-oil with a wipe of raw linseed oil once every few months or when the surface feels dry.

attrs.intro: Problems specific to cherry and small-bowl hook-knife work.

items.fix: Rebuild the edge on a 1,000-grit waterstone: two minutes of circular honing on the flat face and the convex face of the blade, then strop. Work the cherry at a slower pace with lighter pull strokes.
items.cause: Cherry is harder and more brittle than sycamore green; the hook knife meets more resistance on the pull stroke and the thin edge chips on a hard spot.
items.symptom: The hook knife edge chips rather than dulling smoothly

items.fix: Mark the bowl with pencil dots at four points around the rim and aim to reach each dot in the same number of strokes. Check by backlighting every few minutes.
items.cause: The hook knife is removing more material on one side of the bowl than the other because the wrist rotation is not even.
items.symptom: The bowl wall is thick on one side and thin on the other

items.fix: The stain is cosmetic only. Wipe iron tools with a dry cloth after use. For future projects, keep a clean dry cloth on the bench and wipe the blade clean between cuts.
items.cause: Cherry reacts with iron: any iron-containing substance on the blade surface transfers a dark tannin stain onto the wood.
items.symptom: The dried spoon has a dark streak across the handle

attrs.heading: Common problems
